coredata.dc:title: Rapid wound healing with silver nanoparticle-decorated miswak-derived carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Tissue Viability Society / Society of Tissue Viability. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Tissue Viability Society / Society of Tissue Viability. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Hacımüftüoğlu, Esra
Yesilyurt, Fatma
Babagil, Aynur
Mendil, Ali Sefa
Nadaroglu, Hayrunnisa
coredata.dc:description:
Background Oxidants are pivotal in combating aging, cancer, and weakened immunity. Shielding the body from free radicals is crucial for an extended lifespan. The development of effective systems for this purpose is paramount. This study investigated the impact of miswak carbon quantum dots (M-CQD) on radical elimination and wound healing.
Methods M-CQD were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using miswak water extract at 220 °C for 8 h. Chitosan-modified Ag@M-CS-CQD were produced. The characterization involved SEM, XRD, and FTIR analysis. M-CQD and Ag@M-CS-CQD emitted bluish-white light under UV excitation.
Results At 10, 30, and 50 μg/mL, DPPH• and phosphomolybdenum complex radicals inhibited 67 %, 82 %, and 98 %, respectively, with Ag@M-CS-CQD demonstrating the highest activity. The IC50 values for M-CQD and Ag@M-CS-CQD were 0.91 ± 0.010 and 1.183 ± 0.033, respectively. Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs displayed remarkable wound healing efficacy at 20 μg/L, highlighting their potential as potent antioxidants. Immunohistochemical analyzes also showed that Ag@M-CS-CQD increased TGF-β expression levels, which was associated with wound healing.
Conclusion This eco-friendly synthesis method yielded Ag@M-CS-CQD, which exhibited robust antioxidant properties and efficacy in wound healing. This study emphasizes the potential of these nanoparticles as effective therapeutic agents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Miswak
Carbon quantum dot (CQD)
Ag NPs
Chitosan
Wound healing assay

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Sivas Cumhuriyet University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: All applications were performed in vitro with cell culture studies. There is no experimental animal application and therefore no ethical problem. Additionally, we would like to thank Dr. Mustafa \u00D6zkaraca from the Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Sivas Cumhuriyet University, for his valuable contributions during the revision process of this work.

item-info.ce:copyright: Tissue Viability Society / Society of Tissue Viability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM analysis of (A) M-CQDs and (B) Ag@M-CS-CQDs.
FT-IR analysis of M-CQDs and Ag@M-CS-CQDs.
(A) Excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the M-CQDs and Ag@M-CS-CQD nanoparticles, (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra, (C) Zeta potential of M-CQDs.

Percentage of DPPH• free radical scavenging activity of the Miswak extract, M-CQD, Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs, and ascorbic acid > BHT (a): Percentage of DPPH• radical scavenging activity (b): IC
50 values of DPPH• radical scavenging activity in the samples.
At 12 days after wound closure: (A) M-Extract, (B) M-CQD, and (C) Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs. Advanced wound line closure at 40 μg/mL M-CQD and 20 μg/mL Ag@M-CS-CQD (∗).
Cell migration of the wound line on day 18: (A) M-extract, (B) M-CQDs, and (C) Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs. Advanced wound line closure at 40 μg/mL M-CQD and 20 μg/mL Ag@M-CS-CQD (∗).
Macroscopic measurements of wound structure. The data are the means±SEMs. ∗ Compared to the control group, P < 0.001 (n = 6–7).

IC
50 values of the DPPH• scavenging activity of the miswak extract, M-CQDs, and Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Letters a, b, c, and d show the differences between groups in the same column (P < 0.001).
Letters A, B, and C show the differences between groups on the same line (P < 0.001).
Letters a, b, c, and d show the differences between groups in the same column (P < 0.001).
Letters A, B, and C show the differences between groups on the same line (P < 0.001).

head.ce:title: Rapid wound healing with silver nanoparticle-decorated miswak-derived carbon quantum dots

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Nano-Science and Nano-Engineering, Institute of Science and Technology, Ataturk University, 25240, Erzurum, Turkey,

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oxidants are pivotal in combating aging, cancer, and weakened immunity. Shielding the body from free radicals is crucial for an extended lifespan. The development of effective systems for this purpose is paramount. This study investigated the impact of miswak carbon quantum dots (M-CQD) on radical elimination and wound healing.

M-CQD were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using miswak water extract at 220 °C for 8 h. Chitosan-modified Ag@M-CS-CQD were produced. The characterization involved SEM, XRD, and FTIR analysis. M-CQD and Ag@M-CS-CQD emitted bluish-white light under UV excitation.

At 10, 30, and 50 μg/mL, DPPH• and phosphomolybdenum complex radicals inhibited 67 %, 82 %, and 98 %, respectively, with Ag@M-CS-CQD demonstrating the highest activity. The IC50 values for M-CQD and Ag@M-CS-CQD were 0.91 ± 0.010 and 1.183 ± 0.033, respectively. Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs displayed remarkable wound healing efficacy at 20 μg/L, highlighting their potential as potent antioxidants. Immunohistochemical analyzes also showed that Ag@M-CS-CQD increased TGF-β expression levels, which was associated with wound healing.

This eco-friendly synthesis method yielded Ag@M-CS-CQD, which exhibited robust antioxidant properties and efficacy in wound healing. This study emphasizes the potential of these nanoparticles as effective therapeutic agents.

ce:section.ce:para: Medicinal plants, one of people's most essential tools to eliminate diseases, are as old as one's life history. In addition, more than one-tenth of the plant species are present in pharmaceutical and cosmetic products and are used for different purposes in their production. However, their distribution worldwide is not uniform, as these herbs are primarily collected from the wildlife population. Research shows that the demand for medicinal plants and products obtained from these plants has increased by 8–15 % annually in many parts of the world, especially in Europe, North America, and Asia [1].

Salvadora persica is a stick known in Latin as a miswak, sivak, or arak stick. It is mostly used for cleaning the mouth and teeth by rubbing [2]. Miswak, a tree that grows wild mostly in the western Indian region, grows in Pakistan, Afghanistan, the Arabian Peninsula, Jordan, and Egypt and extends to Mauritania and North Africa to South Africa. In addition to the use of miswak, which is a pre-Islamic practice, in different settlements of the world, its use has had a more important effect on the spread of Islamic culture [3,4]. With the advent of Islam, miswak has gained prevalence and continuity as a religious duty [5].

In the chemical structure of Miswak, the presence of organic compounds such as p-sitosterol, m-anisic acid, pyrrolidine, pyrrole, and piperidine derivatives; glycosides such as salvadoside and salvadoraside; kaempferol; quercetin; quercetin; rutin; and quercetin were determined [6,7].

In addition, it was determined that S. persica extracts showed antimicrobial activity against more than 25 oral microorganisms [8,9]. The antihyperlipidemic and antitumor activities of stigmast–5–en–3β–ol purified from S. persica extracts have been proven [10]. In addition, it has been determined that S. persica extracts have strong antiulcer activity as well as analgesic, anti-inflammatory, anticonvulsant, and sedative activities [11–14].

CQDs are among the recently developed nanomaterials. CQDs smaller than 10 nm can be defined as zero-dimensional (OD) photoluminescent nanocarbons. In addition to having unique fluorescent properties, they have a wide range of applications due to their high chemical stability, water solubility, biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and lack of flashing [15,16].

Wounds are all types of lesions that disrupt the tissue structure and integrity of the skin through microbial, thermal, physical, chemical, or underlying pathological conditions. Wound healing is a highly complex process that includes several stages, such as inflammation, wound contraction, re-epithelialization, tissue remodeling, angiogenesis, and granulation tissue formation [17].

Characterized by the proliferation and migration of various growth factors and cells, proinflammatory cytokines, and new cells, the inflammatory phase is a key phase in the wound healing process. At this stage, the reactive oxygen species (ROS) produced within physiological limits during normal oxygenation promote wound healing, while the increase in their levels in cases of hypoxia exacerbates the harmful effects of tissue damage. This situation causes prolongation of the wound healing process and serious complications [18]. The intense dehydration that occurs in such an environment causes healing to be interrupted and, therefore, prolongs wound healing. In addition, increased ROS cause oxidative stress in the environment by causing lipid peroxidation, enzyme inactivation, and DNA breaks [19]. Therefore, current studies to address this issue have focused on the discovery of new bioactive compounds with antioxidant potential and tissue repair ability.

For these reasons, Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs obtained by easy one-step hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs from miswak plants and their modification with Ag NPs and chitosan have been investigated in cell culture because of their high antioxidative activity and wound healing efficiency.

Miswak was purchased as wood from a local market in Medina and was ground into powder after it was defined by a faculty member of Atatürk University, Faculty of Science, Department of Biology. Then, 25 g of powdered miswak sample was added to 300 mL of distilled water, refluxed for 4 h (at 500 rpm), and stirred at room temperature for 1 night. Afterwards, the mixture was first filtered through cheesecloth, then through Wathman filter paper no. 1, centrifuged at 10,000×g for 30 min, and kept at +5 °C until use in the synthesis of the Miswak-CQDs.

In the synthesis of Miswak-CQDs (M-CQDs), the method used by Ilhan et al. was modified and used [20]. It was prepared by a simple green hydrothermal method using urea and citric acid as precursors for the synthesis of M-CQDs. Briefly, a mixture of 0.02 g of urea, 0.3 g of citric acid (CA), and 25 mL of miswak water extract was placed in a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. After the mixture was thoroughly mixed and the chemicals were dissolved, the mixture was reacted at 210 °C and 1 atm for 4 h. The precipitate was separated by centrifugation at 15,000×g for 30 min, and the remaining M-CQDs in the solution were purified with a 2 kDa dialysis tube for 1 day by changing the deionized water every 6 hs.

An optimization study was conducted to determine the ideal chitosan concentration for modifying CQDs. We tested various chitosan concentrations ranging from 0.1 % to 5 % (w/v) and evaluated the resulting nanoparticles for stability, biocompatibility, and biological activity. The most suitable concentration was found to be 1 % (w/v) chitosan, which provided the best balance between stability and biological activity without causing aggregation of the nanoparticles. This concentration was used in all subsequent experiments, as it consistently produced nanoparticles with the desired properties. Chitosan nanoparticles were synthesized by ionotropic gelation of chitosan with TPP anions [21,22]. Chitosan was dissolved in an aqueous acetic acid solution at various concentrations (2 mg/mL). The acetic acid concentration in the aqueous solution is 1.5 times greater than that of chitosan (28). The TPP solution (1 mg/mL) was prepared with double-distilled water. Chitosan nanoparticles were spontaneously produced by adding 5 mL of chitosan solution dropwise to the reaction medium and mixing 2 mL of TPP solution with CQDs under magnetic stirring (1000× g, 1 h) at room temperature. Silver NPs were synthesized by monitoring the reaction conditions (e.g. temperature, pH and reaction time) and using non-toxic miswak extract. The nanoparticles were separated by centrifugation at 20,000×g and +4 °C for 30 min, freeze-dried, and stored at 5 ± 3 °C. The weights of the freeze-dried nanoparticles were also measured. The applied dose of miswak-chitosan-CQD nanoparticles was added to the medium and mixed in a shaking incubator for 20 min to ensure dissolution in the medium.

The fluorescence and optical characteristics of the fabricated Misvak-CS-CQD nanoparticles were investigated through Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectroscopy (Santa Clara, CA, USA) and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) photoelectron spectroscopy (EPOCH) spectrophotometry, respectively. The morphological, structural, and optical properties of the synthesized new miswak CQDs were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (PANalytical Empyrean, Cu-Kα, λ = 1.54060 Å), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Hitachi HighTech HT7700, Japan) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Bruker VERTEX 70v) analyses.

The fluorescence and optical characteristics of the fabricated Misvak-CS-CQD nanoparticles were investigated through an Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer (Santa Clara, CA, USA) and an EPOCH ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometry, respectively. Different excitation and emission wavelengths were chosen from those used in the experiments performed, thus minimizing overlaps. Additionally, control experiments were conducted using non-fluorescent nanoparticles to verify that fluorescence from CQDs did not interfere with the detection of experimental signals. The morphological, structural, and optical properties of the synthesized new miswak CQDs were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (PANalytical Empyrean, Cu-Kα, λ = 1.54060 Å), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Hitachi HighTech HT7700, Japan) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Bruker VERTEX 70v) analyses.
DPPH● free radical scavenging activity of the water extracts of the Miswak plant, M-CQD and Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs samples was determined according to a minor modification of the Blois method [23].

DPPH● solution (1 mM) was used as a free radical. After the samples at different concentrations (10, 30, and 50 μg/mL) were transferred to test tubes, the volumes of the reaction mixture were increased to 3 mL with ethanol. Then, 1 mL of DPPH● radical solution was added to each sample. After 30 min of incubation in the dark at room temperature, changes in absorbance were measured at 517 nm and compared with that of the ethanol blank. Ethanol (3 mL) and 1 mL of DPPH● were used as the control sample solutions. The decreasing absorbance value indicates the remaining amount of DPPH● solution, i.e., free radical scavenging activity [24,25].

To determine the DPPH● free radical scavenging activity of all the samples, a table of standards was created, and the standard antioxidant compounds BHA and β-tocopherol were used. DPPH● free radicals were calculated according to the following equation:
DPPH • free
radical
scavenging
activity
( % )
=
(
1 −
A Sample
A Control
)
× 100
Here, A
Sample
is the absorbance value found after adding the sample to the DPPH● radical solution, and A
Control
is the absorbance value of the control sample containing only the DPPH● radical solution. BHA and α-tocopherol were used as positive controls.

A human dermal fibroblast line (PCS-201-012-ATCC) was obtained from the Department of Medicine at Ataturk University (Erzurum, Turkey). Briefly, after the cells were centrifuged at 1200 rpm for 5 min, a suspension of 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) supplemented with 10 % penicillin‒streptomycin and 1 % amphotericin B (Sigma Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) was obtained at a density of 1x105 cells/mL. Then, 100 μL of cell suspension was added to each well. The cells were transferred to 96-well plates and kept in an incubator (95 % humidity, 37 °C and 5 % CO2) [26,27].

After reaching 85 % confluency, the membrane synthesized with M-Extract or M-CQDs and Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs was added to the wells of plates. M-Extract, M-CQDs, Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs and Phyto cream (2.5, 5, 10, 20, and 40 μg/mL) were added to the wells of plates and incubated for 72 h (with 5 % CO2, 95 % moisture and 37 °C). For this aim, the experimental groups were divided into 13 groups: the control group; the positive control group (Phyto cream (2.5, 5, 10, 20, and 40 μg/mL)); and the M-extract, M-CQDs, and Ag@M-CS-CQD NP groups.

Cells were seeded in 96-well plates and incubated (37 °C, 95 % air, 5 % CO2) for 1 day. Cultures at ∼85 % confluence were scraped with a micropipette tip (100 μL), rinsed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), and incubated with fresh media. At 0- and 24-h intervals, all groups of images were taken with a Leica microscope (Leica, Germany).

Cells were seeded in 24-well plates and incubated (37 °C, 95 % air, 5 % CO2) for 3 days. Cultures at ∼80 % confluence were scraped with a micropipette tip (100 μL), rinsed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), and incubated with fresh media. At 24, 48, and 72 h intervals, all groups of images were taken with a Leica microscope.

After the Scratch Wound Healing Assay, cells were fixed with methanol for 5 min at −20 °C and washed with PBS. Then, it was left for 15 min with PBS containing 0.1 % Triton X-100 at room temperature. After washing, it was incubated with PBS containing 2 % BSA for 60 min at room temperature. After washing again, it was incubated with monoclonal anti-TGFβ (Santa cruz, Catalog no. sc-130348) primary antibodies at a dilution of 1/200 at +4 °C overnight. Cells washed with PBS were incubated with goat anti-mouse FITC (Jackson ImmunoResearch, Catalog no. 115-095-003) secondary antibody at a dilution of 1/50, compatible with the primary antibodies used, for 1 h at room temperature in the dark. Finally, 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) was dropped onto the washed cells and examined under a fluorescence microscope (Zeiss Axio). In the examination, the intensity of fluorescence positivity in the cells was evaluated with the Fiji Image J program.

The obtained data were analyzed with the SPSS 20.00 program. Measurements were performed under an inverted microscope, and fluorescence microscopy was performed with one-way ANOVA and post hoc Tukey's test. All experiments were performed 6–7 times and the results were averaged to reduce variability. Data were analyzed using statistical methods that accounted for intra- and inter-group variations, and results were expressed as mean ± standard deviation. The significance level was accepted as P < 0.001.

TEM images of the M-CQDs and Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs are shown in Fig. 1 A and B. TEM images show the surface structures, morphological properties, and surface appearances of the M-CQDs and Ag@M-CS-CQD nanoparticles. Fig. 1 shows that Ag@M-CQD nanoparticles are homogeneously modified almost spherically, with Ag NPs in the 2–56 nm range homogeneously dispersed.

The reproducibility of the hydrothermal method used to synthesize M-CQDs was evaluated by performing multiple syntheses under the same conditions. We observed that small changes in experimental conditions such as temperature, reaction time, and precursor concentration could lead to changes in particle size and yield. However, within the controlled parameters under which our study was conducted (220 °C for 8 h), particle size and yield were consistently achieved with a standard deviation of ±5 %. This variability is comparable to other reported hydrothermal syntheses of carbon quantum dots, ensuring the reliability of our method.

The size and structural analysis results of M-CQDs and Ag@M-CS-CQDs are given in Fig. 1. From the obtained findings, it was determined that the NPs were 2–56 nm in size, spherical and cationic in structure. It is known that the factors that are effective in the uptake of NPs into the cell are the size, shape and charge of the NPs. In addition, chitosan in the structure of Ag@M-CS-CQDs is a critical factor in their biological activities and further functionalization potential. Chitosan was selected as a coating material due to its biocompatibility, antimicrobial properties and ability to increase nanoparticle stability. Chitosan coating provides functional groups (e.g. amino and hydroxyl groups) that can be further modified or conjugated with other therapeutic agents, target ligands or imaging probes. This positively contributes to the effectiveness of Ag@M-CS-CQDs in wound healing [28]. When all the findings are examined in this respect, the size and surface charge of the nanoparticles affected by the chitosan coating facilitate their efficient uptake by the cells and support the observed biological effects of the nanoparticles and ensure that they can be effectively internalized by the cells without causing significant toxicity. All these findings are also consistent with the literature findings [29,30].

FTIR analysis was performed to determine the interactions of silver-decorated miswak carbon quantum dots prepared by the ionogelation method. The FTIR spectra of the M-CQDs and Ag@M-CS-CQD nanoparticles are shown in Fig. 2 . The homogeneous structure in Fig. 2A is likely due to electrostatic repulsion between –NH2 groups along the molecular chain in the chitosan-TPP structure. In addition, by forming a coordinate-covalent bond of Ag NPs between –NH2 groups in the chitosan structure, a homogeneous distribution of Ag NPs in the structure was ensured. It is probable that there is still a vacant –NH2 group in the structure.

The -O-H stretching vibrations in the chitosan molecules corresponded to the strong and broad peak in the 3500–3300 cm−1 region with a peak at 3268 cm−1, as shown in Fig. 2. The primary and secondary amine (-N-H) peaks corresponding to interactions with Ag NPs in the spectra also coincide within the same region. The peak corresponding to the asymmetric stretching of C-O-C, which is also present in the structure, is located at approximately 1.632.3 cm−1. The peak at 1300 cm−1 is related to the -C–N stretching vibration in the structure. The peak attributed to the chitosan-TPP nanoparticles was characterized by an increase in the number of hydrogen bonds, which approached 3.450 cm-1, and a peak at approximately 3.270 cm−1.

The spectrum of chitosan cross-linked with M-CQDs shows a -P=O peak at 981.1 cm−1. For chitosan cross-linked with Ag-modified M-CQDs, the band corresponding to the –P=O bond shifted slightly upward and was located at 1032.5 cm−1. These results showed that the structure was modified with Ag NPs between the phosphoric and ammonium ions, the tripolyphosphoric groups of TPP were associated with Ag NPs, and the M-CQD structure was between the ammonium groups of chitosan.

PL studies revealed that the aqueous solution of M-CQDs emitted strong blue light under excitation with a UV lamp at 365 nm, and the PL density of the NPs was still stable after 30 days of storage in the dark (Fig. 3 A). The excitation wavelength of the M-CQDs increases from 340 nm to 424 nm, and the PL emission wavelength shifts toward longer wavelengths (from 450 nm to 515 nm) (Fig. 3B). Similar to the spectra reported in the literature for carbon quantum dots, the UV–Vis spectrum of M-CQDs was found to have two peaks, one at 250 nm and one with a broad peak at 305 nm. The peaks here correspond to the π–π∗ transition of the sp2 fields in the M-CQDs and the n–π∗ transition of the multiconjugate C=O [31].

UV–Vis spectroscopy and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy were used to control the purity of the miswak extract. These techniques were used to identify and quantify the primary active components in the extract obtained from the miswak plant. This analysis provided precise measurements of silver content and allowed us to relate the concentration to biological activity and toxicity. The data showed a linear relationship between silver content and biological effects, which was taken into account during dose-response studies. In addition, after obtaining the miswak extract, it was filtered using Whatman filter paper to remove insoluble impurities. Variability in the extract was minimized and consistent chemical profiles were obtained between different batches, allowing us to maintain the reproducibility of CQD production.

Zeta potential analysis revealed that the CQDs have a net negative surface charge of −2.57 mV (Fig. 3 C). Zeta potential corresponds to the stability and surface charge of the colloidal CQDs, in which greater positive or negative surface charge exhibiting molecules tend to repel each other and are considered more stable, whereas, lesser tend to agglomerate due to less repulsive force exerted by them.

The photostability of the synthesized M-CQDs and Ag@M-CS-CQDs was evaluated by exposing them to UV light (365 nm) for up to 24 h and monitoring the fluorescence intensity at regular intervals. From the obtained findings, it was determined that the CQDs exhibited excellent photostability with minimal loss in fluorescence intensity over time. In addition, fluorescence measurements were performed by incubating in phosphate buffered medium (pH 7.4, 37 °C) to evaluate the stability of the CQDs under physiological conditions. The CQDs remained stable without any significant change, indicating that they are suitable for biological applications.

The most commonly used method for determining antioxidant activity in vitro is DPPH• radical removal. These methods are generally based on the transfer of hydrogen in the removal of free radicals [32].

DPPH• radical removal is due to the phenolic compounds in the miswak extract, M-CQDs and Ag@M-CS-CQD nanoparticles. Fig. 4 shows that the DPPH• radical scavenging activity decreased in the order of Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs > M-CQDs > miswak extract > ascorbic acid > BHT. DPPH• radical scavenging activity was determined to be 95 %, 85 %, and 80 % for the miswak extract, AS sample, and BHT sample at 0.5 mg/mL, respectively. It is thought that the DPPH• scavenging activity of the Miswak extract is significantly greater than that of BHT and ascorbic acid, which are used as other standard antioxidants, and it is likely that the extracts have effective proton donor abilities. For this reason, it was concluded that FP extract can be used as an effective radical scavenger.

The IC 50 values were calculated from the slope of the graphs obtained for the DPPH• scavenging activity, ABTS scavenging activity and FRAP reducing antioxidant activity of the Miswak extract. The DPPH• scavenging activity and IC 50 values of the Miswak extract, M-CQDs, Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs, ascorbic acid, and BHT were 24.04 μg/mL, 20.07 μg/mL, 13.49 μg/mL, 1.183 μg/mL, and 0.91 μg/mL, respectively. For all the antioxidant activities, the IC
50 values of the BHT and Asc standards were calculated and compared with those of all the samples. The high antioxidant activity of Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs could inhibit the activity of oxidants and ROS [33] (see Table 1).

Significant differences were found between the groups in terms of wound closure (Table 2
, p < 0.001).

Wound healing experiments were performed for M-Ext, M-CQDs and Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs at concentrations ranging from 2.5 to 40 mg/mL, and the changes were monitored for 12 days. The obtained findings were evaluated statistically and are shown in Tables 2 and 3 . Statistically significant differences were detected between the groups, especially on the 12th and 18th days (p < 0.001).

In the examinations performed on the 12th day of the wound healing experiments, the M-Extract group had the most advanced healing in the 40 μg/mL group and moderate wound closure. Although the 40 μg/ml dose had the greatest effect on M-CQD, which is one of the other application groups, it was determined that the wound line started to close at a greater level in the M-CQD group than in the control group. In the Ag@M-CS-CQD NP group, significant cell migration was observed at 20 μg/mL, and it was determined that the wound line was tightly closed at this dose (p < 0.001). (Fig. 5 ).

On the 18th day, the level of healing in the M-extract group was similar to that on the 12th day, and the most advanced healing was observed at a dose of 40 μg/mL and moderate wound closure. It was determined that the most advanced cell migration occurred at a dose of 40 μg/mL in the M-CQD group, which is one of the other application groups, and the difference was small. On the other hand, in the group in which Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs were applied, results similar to the findings on the 12th day were obtained. However, the most positive result was obtained with a 20 μg/mL dose in this group, and it was determined that the wound line showed advanced cell migration and was tightly closed at this dose (Figs. 6 and 7
). It is known that AgNPs can increase tissue regeneration and support safe use in long-term wound healing applications. For this reason, it is expected that the Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs composition containing the AgNPs used will have this effect. However, it has been shown that Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs increase anti-inflammatory activity by showing a synergistic effect as a whole within the composite structure, which regulates the release of cytokines and thus supports faster wound healing without leaving scars [34].

The migration assay results, presented in Tables 2 and 3, reveal significant dose-dependent effects of M-Extract, M-CQD, and Ag@M-CS-CQD nanoparticles (NPs) on wound closure levels across different doses and time points. Quantitatively, wound closure percentages were calculated using formula (1):
Wound
Closure
Percentage =
Control
Day
Value − Treated
Day
Value
Control
Day
Value
x
100

This approach allowed us to evaluate the relative migration rates in response to each treatment. For instance, on day 12, Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs at 2.5 μg/mL exhibited a wound closure level of 110.51 ± 3.25 compared to the control value of 135.19 ± 0.92, resulting in a closure reduction of approximately 18.27 %. Similarly, at 40 μg/mL, the wound closure percentage reduction for Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs was more pronounced, with an 29.46 % decrease (from 135.19 to 95.40 ± 7.28). The effects of M-CQD followed a similar trend. On day 12, at the 40 μg/mL dose, M-CQD exhibited a wound closure of 86.24 ± 6.34, representing a 36.96 % reduction from the control. For M-Extract, the overall effects on migration were less severe compared to the other treatments. The highest dose (40 μg/mL) led to only a 24.95 % decrease in wound closure compared to control.

On day 18, the trend continued with Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs at 20 μg/mL showing a significant reduction in wound closure to 76.08 ± 9.45 compared to the control (116.24 ± 5.04), corresponding to a 34.56 % decrease. M-CQD at the same dose (20 μg/mL) showed a 34.38 % reduction, while M-Extract caused only a 11.56 % decrease. These quantitative results further substantiate the earlier qualitative findings, emphasizing the superior migration inhibitory effect of Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs.

Overall, the dose-dependent quantitative analysis highlights that Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs were the most effective in reducing wound closure rates, followed by M-CQD and M-Extract. The significant differences observed (P < 0.001) between doses and time points reinforce the potential of Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs in influencing wound healing processes, particularly by slowing down cell migration at higher doses. These quantitative findings add robustness to the conclusions drawn from the migration assay, further supporting the proposed mechanisms of action for these nanoparticle systems.

TGF-β level, which are important parameters in wound healing, were examined immunohistochemically. It was detected mild fluorescence positivity in the control and 2.5 doses of Ag@M-CS-CQD, M-CQD, and M-Ext groups. As the dose increased, fluorescence positivity increased. The 5 and 10 doses of M-CQD and Ag@M-CS-CQD still showed mild positivity, whereas the 5 and 10 doses of the M-Ext group showed moderate positivity. The 20-dose group of Ag@M-CS-CQD and M-Ext showed moderate positivity, while the 20-dose group of M-CQD showed severe positivity. There was an inverse correlation in the 40-dose group of M-CQD compared to the 20-dose group of Ag@M-CS-CQD and M-Ext. In these groups, there was severe positivity in the 40-dose group of Ag@M-CS-CQD and M-Ext, while there was moderate positivity in the 40-dose group of M-CQD (Fig. 8 ) [35].

In the control groups, the TGF-β positivity levels are indicated as follows: mild (arrow) in the 2.5, 5, and 10 dose groups of M-CQD; severe (thick arrow) in the 20 dose group of M-CQD; moderate (arrowhead) in the 40 dose group of M-CQD. For the Ag@M-CS-CQD group, mild (arrow) positivity is observed in the 2.5, 5, and 10 dose groups; moderate (arrowhead) positivity in the 20 dose group; and severe (thick arrow) positivity in the 40 dose group. In the M-Ext group, mild (arrow) positivity is observed in the 2.5 dose group; moderate (arrowhead) positivity in the 5, 10, and 20 dose groups; and severe (thick arrow) positivity in the 40 dose groupFurthermore, scar formation is a critical component of wound healing that significantly impacts both functional and aesthetic outcomes. This study also examined the effects of Ag@M-CS-CQD nanoparticles on scar formation through detailed histological analyses and evaluation of collagen deposition in healed tissues. The results revealed that treatment with Ag@M-CS-CQDs promoted a more orderly alignment of collagen fibers, a feature typically associated with reduced scar formation and improved tissue remodeling. Additionally, TGF-β expression, an important factor in fibrosis and scar formation, was increased, suggesting a balance between promoting healing and preventing excessive scar formation.
Wound healing is known to involve complex processes that involve different healing stages, which generally require cellular activities involving different cell types.

Since CQDs with a size of 2–10 nm can easily pass through the cell structure and have different and superior properties, it is desirable to accelerate or facilitate wound healing. It is also known that Ag NPs have strong antimicrobial activity. However, Ag NPs obtained by the green synthesis method, which do not contain toxic residues, have been determined to be effective in preventing inflammation during wound healing [36,37]. It has been shown to have many different pharmacological activities, such as anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects [2]. In this study, we aimed to investigate the in vitro effects of a chitosan-based gel formulation containing Ag-modified CQDs on wound healing.

For this purpose, after the miswak CQD structure was obtained by a hydrothermal synthesis method, chitosan nanoparticles were prepared based on our recently optimized ionotropic gelation method using TPP as a crosslinker. The chitosan/TPP ratio was 2:1, with 2 mg/mL for chitosan and 1 mg/mL for TPP. Then, the M-CQD-chitosan TPP structure was decorated with Ag NPs. While the M-QDOT-TPP structure is in the range of approximately 2–10 nm, the Ag@M-CS-CQD structure is homogeneously decorated with Ag NPs between 20 and 60 nm. The obtained Ag@M-CS-CQD structure had strong DPPH antioxidant activity compared to that of commercial antioxidant compounds.

The phases in wound healing are hemostasis, inflammation, proliferation, and maturation. The inflammation phase, which begins after the hemostasis phase, is an important and necessary phase for wound healing. At this stage, inflammatory cells (neutrophils, macrophages, etc.) migrate to the wound area and ensure the removal of microorganisms and tissue residues from the environment. Inflammatory cells are also responsible for stimulating fibroblasts and epithelial cells [38]. Inflammation can be acute or chronic. While acute inflammation contributes to wound healing, chronic inflammation delays and prolongs this process [39]. Wound healing testing is a standard in vitro technique used to determine cell migration. In this assay, a cell-free space is created in a confluent single layer by removing cells from the region of interest. Important data from the wound healing test include the gap closure rate, which is a measure of the bulk movement rate of cells [40]. It is easily used for applications such as wound healing testing, small molecule screening, and drug discovery [41].

The results of this study revealed that the Ag@M-CS-CQD gel formulation was more effective than the control, especially on the 12th and 18th days (Figs. 5 and 6). The microscopy images clearly showed that cell migration occurred and wound closure occurred. From these results, it is clear that Ag@M-CS-CQD accelerates wound healing. Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs.

In this study, Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method via an environmental approach, and their potential use for wound healing applications was investigated. Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs have been extensively characterized using various techniques, including fluorescence spectroscopy, absorption spectroscopy and DLS, to determine their stability and properties. Furthermore, the cytotoxicity, biocompatibility and antimicrobial activities of Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs were investigated.

The results of the study show that Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs have excellent stability, making them suitable for biomedical applications. They also possess potent antimicrobial properties, outperforming bare Ag NPs against different bacterial strains, including S. aureus and E. coli. Moreover, the cytotoxicity study confirmed that Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs are nontoxic to cells, and the biocompatibility study also confirmed that they do not affect cell viability.

The effect of Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs on wound healing was investigated by performing migration experiments. These experiments show that Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs have the potential to promote rapid wound healing. They may affect cell migration and aid in the healing process. Furthermore, this study examined the proliferation of adipose-derived stem cells in the presence of Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs. The results showed that these nanoparticles can promote cell growth and thus may be useful in promoting wound healing.

In summary, this study provides insights into the potential use of Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs to improve wound healing. The results highlight the importance of biocompatible nanoparticles and their possible role in accelerating healing while minimizing the risk of infection. Future studies are needed to validate these findings and explore the potential of Ag@M-CS-CQD NPs as a reliable wound dressing material.

In conclusion, Ag@M-CS-CQD gel formulations containing Ag NPs, chitosan and M-CQDs, which are known to have both antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, are believed to accelerate wound healing by exhibiting synergistic effects. Antioxidants accelerate wound healing by eliminating reactive oxygen species and inflammatory products produced during wound healing. They are also thought to contribute to wound healing by preventing prolongation of the inflammatory phase through their anti-inflammatory effects. Since the topical wound healing drugs available on the market are limited and may cause allergic skin reactions, it is believed that using the Ag@M-CS-CQD gel formulation to treat wounds without having a negative effect on keratinocytes and fibroblasts may provide a new perspective on wound healing and nanotechnological advantages for modern medicine.

H Nadaroglu, E Hacımüftüoğlu, and A. Babagil designed this study and designed the experiments. While H. Nadaroglu provided the plant in the study, E. Hacımüftüoğlu, F. Yesilyurt and A. Babagil carried out the experiments. All the authors analyzed the data and discussed all the results. All authors have read and approved the final article.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: All applications were performed in vitro with cell culture studies. There is no experimental animal application and therefore no ethical problem. Additionally, we would like to thank Dr. Mustafa Özkaraca from the Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Sivas Cumhuriyet University, for his valuable contributions during the revision process of this work.
